body_txt: More often than not, changes are made when working on an adaptation. Sometimes it's characterization, that changes their behavior. Others, it's modifications to their hair, eye, or clothing color. And of course, one thing that gets changed regularly is the characters' costumes. This could be because of a new setting from canon, an alternate universe that requires new outfits, outfits being unable to be recreated easily in live-action or animation, or the creator of an adaptation wanting to add some Costume Porn . Supertropes to Adaptational Modesty (a character's outfit is less revealing in the adaptation), Adaptational Skimpiness (a character's outfit is more revealing in the adaptation), Adaptation Dye-Job (a character's outfit or hair is a different color in the adaptation), Culture Equals Costume (if the character gets a Race Lift as well), and Movie Superheroes Wear Black (live-action adaptations give superheroes monochromatic costumes, where they previously wear colored costumes). Occasionally, the original outfit can appear as a Mythology Gag to the source material. Extreme cases may lead to You Don't Look Like You . At times, the reasoning behind the costume change of characters is one of merchandising , where action figures may have different outfits in order to tie them to a particular adaptation and not seem outdated. Other times the original outfit was a case of Awesome, but Impractical to translate into live-action. Examples where characters in-universe alter what they wear don't apply here — that's Significant Wardrobe Shift . NOTE: Only significant changes to costuming in between the original work and the adaptation should be listed — major changes in color, the removal of iconic accessories, alterations to the overall silhouette of the costume or the materials it's made out of, or major changes to things such as hairstyling all count. Something like a character having a slightly different color of button or hair ribbon does not.
